name: IRS Publication 4812 — Contractor Security Controls
description: Publication 4812 (Contractor Security & Privacy Controls) defines security and privacy requirements for contractors handling IRS Sensitive But Unclassified data, including Federal Tax Information — based on the NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 moderate baseline with IRS-specific tailoring. Note on version markings: the IRS revision format is month-year, so Rev. 12-2024 means the December 2024 revision and Rev. 12-2025 the December 2025 revision.

highlights: Publication renamed 'Contractor Security Controls' → 'Contractor Security & Privacy Controls', reflecting full privacy integration
Control baseline moved from NIST SP 800-53 Revision 3 to Revision 5, adding the PT (PII Processing and Transparency) and SR (Supply Chain Risk Management) families
Brand-new Cloud Computing section: no cloud use without Contracting Officer and IRS Cybersecurity approval; FTI restricted to FedRAMP Moderate/High environments
Brand-new Artificial Intelligence section: AI policy required; entering SBU/FTI into public AI tools (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) is treated as an intentional unauthorized disclosure and data breach
Contractor Security Reviews formalized into the CSA (Contractor Security Assessment) program with tiered NET / SOFT / C-SCRM control levels

changes.title: Publication renamed: 'Contractor Security Controls' → 'Contractor Security & Privacy Controls'
changes.oldText: Publication 4812 — Contractor Security Controls. ***This Publication Pertains to IT Assets Owned and Managed at Contractor Sites***
changes.newText: Publication 4812 — Contractor Security & Privacy Controls. ***This Publication Pertains to IT Assets Owned, Managed, or Operated by IRS Contractors***
changes.rationale: Over twelve years the publication evolved from a security-only controls document into an integrated security and privacy controls framework, and its scope statement broadened from assets 'at contractor sites' to any assets owned, managed, or operated by IRS contractors.

changes.title: NIST SP 800-53 baseline updated: Revision 3 → Revision 5
changes.oldText: As described in NIST SP 800-53 (Revision 3), as amended... the security controls and requirements herein are based on NIST SP 800-53 (Revision 3), as amended.
changes.newText: The security controls and requirements hereinafter are based on NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5.
changes.rationale: The original 2013 publication tailored the NIST SP 800-53 Revision 3 moderate baseline. The current publication is aligned to Revision 5, which merged privacy controls into the main catalog and added the Supply Chain Risk Management family — both reflected throughout Publication 4812's structure.

changes.title: New Section 6.0 — Cloud Computing
changes.newText: Contractors must not employ Cloud computing services to support the IRS contract without approval from the Contracting Officer and IRS Cybersecurity approval. FTI may only be stored, processed, or handled in FedRAMP Authorized cloud environments operating at the Moderate or High impact level.
changes.rationale: The 2013 publication contained no mention of cloud computing — it assumed an on-premise contractor IT model. The dedicated cloud section requires CO and IRS Cybersecurity approval before any cloud use and restricts FTI to FedRAMP Authorized environments at Moderate or High impact level.

changes.title: New Section 7.0 — Artificial Intelligence
changes.newText: The contractor must define an AI policy for any design, development, acquisition, or use of AI... Contractors are strictly prohibited from disclosing Sensitive but Unclassified (SBU), to include PII and Federal Tax Information (FTI) information, to publicly available generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Google Bard/Gemini, Anthropic Claude)... The IRS considers improperly sharing data with such an AI or internet tool as an intentional unauthorized disclosure and data breach. Contractors and subcontractors that would like to implement AI technologies within their environments that support the IRS must receive approval by the CO before implementing AI.
changes.rationale: An entirely new requirement area with no 2013 counterpart. Covers GenAI, predictive AI, ML, LLMs, chatbots, and RPA. Entering FTI or SBU into public AI tools is treated as an intentional unauthorized disclosure and data breach.

changes.title: Disclosure section elevated to 'Unauthorized Access (UNAX) and Disclosure of Information'
changes.oldText: 6 Disclosure of Information
changes.newText: 8.0 Unauthorized Access (UNAX) and Disclosure of Information
changes.rationale: The 2013 publication had a brief 'Disclosure of Information' section. The current version elevates UNAX — willful unauthorized access or inspection of taxpayer data — into the section title and expands the treatment of criminal and civil penalties under IRC §7213, §7213A, and §7431.

changes.title: 'Contractor Security Reviews' became 'Contractor Security Assessments' (CSA program)
changes.oldText: 7 Contractor Security Reviews — 7.1 Overview, 7.2 Types of Reviews, 7.3 Notice of Reviews, 7.4 Scope of Reviews
changes.newText: 10.0 Contractor Security Assessments — 10.1 Overview, 10.2 Types of Assessments, 10.3 Notice of Assessments, 10.4 Security Control Levels, 10.5 Scope of Assessments
changes.rationale: The review program was formalized into the Contractor Security Assessment (CSA) program with a dedicated CSA Team, defined assessment types, and security control levels — the operational core of how the IRS now verifies contractor compliance.

changes.title: IRS roles reorganized: CSA Team and FMSS replace 2013 role structure
changes.oldText: 8.1.3 Information Technology and Cybersecurity/ISR; 8.1.5 Physical Security and Emergency Preparedness
changes.newText: 9.1.3 Contractor Security Assessment Team (CSA); 9.1.5 Facilities Management and Security Services (FMSS)
changes.rationale: Government-side responsibilities were reassigned to match current IRS organization: the CSA Team owns contractor assessment, and Facilities Management and Security Services (FMSS) replaced Physical Security and Emergency Preparedness.

changes.title: Security Control Levels introduced: NET, SOFT, and C-SCRM
changes.newText: Security Control Levels: Networked Information Technology Infrastructure (NET); Software Application Development/Maintenance (SOFT); Cyber Supply Chain Risk Management (C-SCRM)
changes.rationale: The 2013 publication applied one control set to all contractors. The current version tiers requirements by contractor type — networked infrastructure (NET), software development (SOFT), and cyber supply chain risk management (C-SCRM) — so control applicability matches the nature of the work.

changes.title: New control family: PII Processing and Transparency (PT)
changes.newText: 27.0 PII Processing and Transparency (PT)
changes.rationale: The 2013 publication had no privacy control family. The PT family, drawn from NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5, brings PII processing purposes, consent, and privacy notice requirements into contractor obligations — the clearest expression of the publication's expansion from security to security-and-privacy.

changes.title: New control family: Supply Chain Risk Management (SR)
changes.newText: 32.0 Supply Chain Risk Management (SR)
changes.rationale: Supply chain risk was mentioned once in the 2013 publication; the current version references it throughout (26 mentions) and adds the full SR control family — SCRM plans, supplier assessments, component authenticity, and disposal — reflecting the post-SolarWinds threat landscape and NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5.

changes.title: CA family renamed: 'Security Assessment and Authorization' → 'Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring'
changes.oldText: 14 Security Assessment and Authorization (CA) — CA-2 Security Assessments; CA-3 Information System Connections
changes.newText: 16.0 Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring (CA) — CA-2 Control Assessments; CA-3 Information Exchange
changes.rationale: Follows the NIST Rev. 5 renaming. CA-2 became Control Assessments and CA-3 became Information Exchange, broadening from formal system interconnection agreements to all forms of information exchange.

changes.title: CA-8 Penetration Testing added
changes.newText: CA-8 Penetration Testing
changes.rationale: The 2013 CA family stopped at continuous monitoring (CA-7). Penetration testing is now an explicit contractor control requirement.

changes.title: Removed: AC-9 Previous Logon Notification, AC-10 Concurrent Session Control, AC-16 Security Attributes
changes.oldText: 11.9 AC-9 Previous Logon (Access) Notification; 11.10 AC-10 Concurrent Session Control; 11.13 AC-16 Security Attributes
changes.rationale: These controls were dropped from the tailored contractor baseline over successive revisions — they are not selected in the current moderate-baseline tailoring for contractor environments.

changes.title: AC-11 renamed: 'Session Lock' → 'Device Lock'
changes.oldText: AC-11 Session Lock
changes.newText: AC-11 Device Lock
changes.rationale: Follows NIST Rev. 5 terminology — the control covers locking the device, not merely the session.

changes.title: Removed: AU-10 Non-Repudiation and AT-5 Contacts with Security Groups
changes.oldText: 13.10 AU-10 Non-Repudiation; 12.5 AT-5 Contacts with Security Groups and Associations
changes.rationale: AU-10 was removed from the contractor baseline; AT-5 was withdrawn in NIST Rev. 5 (incorporated into PM-15). The AT family was also modernized: AT-2 is now Security Awareness Training and AT-3 Role Based Training.

changes.title: AU-2 renamed: 'Auditable Events' → 'Event Logging'
changes.oldText: 13.2 AU-2 Auditable Events
changes.newText: 15.2 AU-2 Event Logging
changes.rationale: Rev. 5 terminology shift emphasizing the act of logging events rather than merely defining which events are auditable.

changes.title: Configuration Management extended: CM-10, CM-11, CM-12 added
changes.newText: CM-10 Software Usage Restrictions; CM-11 User-Installed Software; CM-12 Information Location
changes.rationale: The 2013 CM family ended at CM-9. The additions govern software licensing restrictions, user-installed software, and — critically for FTI — knowing and controlling where information resides (CM-12).

changes.title: New technical controls across SA, SC, SI, MP, IR, and RA families
changes.newText: SA-15 Development Process, Standards, and Tools; SA-22 Unsupported System Components; SC-39 Process Isolation; SI-16 Memory Protection; MP-7 Media Use; IR-9 Information Spillage Response; RA-9 Criticality Analysis
changes.rationale: A dozen years of baseline evolution added secure-development process requirements, prohibition of unsupported (end-of-life) components, memory protection, media use restrictions, information spillage response, and criticality analysis to the contractor control set.

changes.title: State of Security (SoS) Package and security assurance statements replaced by CSA processes
changes.oldText: 8.2.10 Contractor Statements of Security Assurance; 8.2.11 State of Security Package
changes.newText: Contractor Security Self-Assessments and CSA-driven assessment reporting under Section 10.0
changes.rationale: The 2013 SoS Package and annual Statements of Security Assurance were superseded by the structured CSA program: self-assessment questionnaires against high-risk controls with Met / Not Met determinations and formal assessment reports.

changes.title: Training: generic Security Awareness Training → catalog of eight mandatory briefings
changes.oldText: Ensure all contractor employees who require staff-like access to IRS information or information systems, regardless of their physical location, complete the required Security Awareness Training.
changes.newText: As of July 2025, IRS assigns mandatory briefings to all individuals supporting IRS contracts: Annual Cybersecurity Awareness; Privacy, Information Protection & Disclosure (PIPD); Records Management; Insider Threat Awareness; FMSS Physical Security; IRS Cybersecurity Awareness; Inadvertent Sensitive Information Access; CUI General Awareness; and UNAX. SAT must be completed before access to SBU data is granted.
changes.rationale: Training evolved from a single generic requirement into a defined curriculum, with completion now a precondition for SBU access rather than a parallel obligation.

changes.title: Personnel vetting: 'background investigation' reframed as 'security screening' with defined roles
changes.oldText: All contractors, subcontractors, experts, consultants, and paid/unpaid interns... are subject to a background investigation... Contractors shall ensure all contractor and subcontractor employees... are identified to the IRS at time of award.
changes.newText: Personnel are subject to a security screening comparable to federal employees in the same position sensitivity. The Vendor POC, in collaboration with the COR, must identify all performing personnel (including anyone meeting the staff-like access definition) at award; the Vendor POC, COR, and CO jointly ensure unfavorably adjudicated personnel are immediately removed.
changes.rationale: Same substance, but responsibility is now assigned to named roles (Vendor POC / COR / CO) instead of "the contractor" generally — typical of Rev. 12's precision about who does what.

changes.title: UNAX section spells out the statutory framework and subcontractor authorization
changes.oldText: Brief "Disclosure of Information" section.
changes.newText: Section 8.0 details IRC §7213 (criminal penalties for unauthorized disclosure), §7213A (unauthorized inspection as a misdemeanor), §7431 (civil damages and taxpayer notification), and §6103(n) authority. Before releasing any returns or return information to a subcontractor, the contractor must have WRITTEN authorization from the IRS. Documented employee/subcontractor notification of restrictions and sanctions is required.
changes.rationale: Elevates taxpayer-data misuse from a passing reference to a fully articulated legal exposure section — including the often-missed written-authorization prerequisite for subcontractor data sharing.

changes.title: 'Dash-one' policy flexibility: existing corporate policies can satisfy XX-1 requirements
changes.newText: A contractor does not have to develop a plan specific to each control family if existing formal documents (e.g., HR policies satisfying PS-1) address the material elements of that family's dash-one control, to the satisfaction of the IRS.
changes.rationale: A pragmatic clarification absent in 2013: mature organizations can map existing policies rather than produce IRS-specific paperwork for all 20 families.

changes.title: Contractor Security Representative → Contractor Point of Contact (POC)
changes.oldText: 8.2.3 Contractor Security Representative
changes.newText: 9.2.1 Contractor Point of Contact (POC) — the named vendor role that coordinates security screening, personnel identification, and assessment logistics with the COR and CSA team.
changes.rationale: Role consolidation matching the CSA program structure.

changes.title: Explicit flow-down of security and privacy requirements to subcontractors at any tier
changes.oldText: Requirements applied to contractors and subcontractors supporting the primary contract.
changes.newText: Requirements defined at the contractor level explicitly flow down to subcontractor information systems and personnel; IRS IT Security/FISMA language must be included in solicitations, contracts, and orders directly or through flow-down provisions.
changes.rationale: Closes the gap where lower-tier subcontractors handling SBU escaped contractual security obligations.

changes.title: 'Security Categorization' expanded into 'Privacy and Information Protection'
changes.oldText: 9 Security Categorization
changes.newText: 11.0 Privacy and Information Protection, with security categorization as a subsection (11.1).
changes.rationale: Categorization is now framed within a broader privacy-and-protection context, consistent with the publication's privacy integration.

highlights: Aligned to NIST SP 800-53 Revision 5 — all control references updated from Rev 4 to Rev 5, adding privacy controls and supply chain requirements to FTI protection obligations
Encryption requirements strengthened: FIPS 140-2 references updated to FIPS 140-3, and encryption now required for FTI at rest in addition to FTI in transit
Multi-factor authentication (MFA) now required for ALL access to systems containing FTI — expanded from remote-access-only requirement in Rev. 8
New supply chain risk management requirements added — agencies must now assess and document risks from vendors with access to FTI systems
Incident reporting timeline tightened: FTI breaches must now be reported to IRS within 24 hours of discovery (reduced from 48 hours in Rev. 8)
Cloud and virtual infrastructure guidance added — agencies using cloud services to process FTI must meet FedRAMP Moderate baseline at minimum

changes.title: All control references updated from NIST SP 800-53 Rev 4 to Rev 5
changes.oldText: Agencies must implement the security controls defined in NIST Special Publication 800-53, Revision 4, Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, at the Moderate baseline or higher for all systems processing FTI.
changes.newText: Agencies must implement the security controls defined in NIST Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5, Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations, at the Moderate baseline (as defined in NIST SP 800-53B) or higher for all systems processing, storing, or transmitting FTI.
changes.rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 was finalized in September 2020 and represents a significant update — integrating privacy controls, adding supply chain risk management, and expanding scope beyond federal systems. IRS Rev. 9 aligns FTI safeguarding requirements to Rev 5 to ensure agencies are implementing current security and privacy controls.

changes.title: Encryption required for FTI at rest — not just in transit
changes.oldText: FTI transmitted over networks must be encrypted using FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic modules. Agencies must protect FTI from unauthorized disclosure during transmission.
changes.newText: FTI must be encrypted using FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules both during transmission over networks and while at rest in storage systems, databases, backup media, and portable devices. Encryption of FTI at rest is required regardless of whether the storage medium is within a secure facility.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8 required encryption of FTI in transit but did not explicitly mandate encryption at rest. Rev. 9 closes this gap — a significant improvement given that most FTI breaches involve stored data (stolen laptops, improperly disposed storage media, database exposures). The update to FIPS 140-3 also reflects NIST's 2019 approval of the updated cryptographic module standard.

changes.title: MFA required for ALL FTI system access, not just remote access
changes.oldText: Multi-factor authentication must be implemented for all remote access to information systems that access, process, store, or transmit FTI, including access by privileged and non-privileged users.
changes.newText: Multi-factor authentication must be implemented for all access to information systems that access, process, store, or transmit FTI — including local network access, privileged access, and non-privileged access. MFA is required regardless of whether the user is accessing the system remotely or from within the agency's internal network.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8 required MFA only for remote access, consistent with the Rev 4-era NIST guidance. Rev. 9 expands this to all access — including from inside the network — aligning with zero-trust principles and reflecting the reality that insider threats and compromised internal accounts are primary vectors for FTI exfiltration.

changes.title: FTI breach reporting timeline reduced from 48 to 24 hours
changes.oldText: Agencies must report suspected or confirmed FTI breaches to the IRS Office of Safeguards within 48 hours of discovery. The report must include a description of the incident, the types of FTI involved, the number of taxpayers affected, and the steps being taken to contain the breach.
changes.newText: Agencies must report suspected or confirmed FTI breaches to the IRS Office of Safeguards within 24 hours of discovery. The initial report must include all known details about the incident at the time of reporting, with updates provided as additional information becomes available. Agencies must also notify affected individuals in accordance with applicable federal and state breach notification laws.
changes.rationale: The 48-hour reporting window was increasingly out of step with federal standards — the CISA and FBI joint guidance recommends immediate reporting, and many states now require breach notification within 24-72 hours. Rev. 9 tightens the IRS requirement to 24 hours and adds explicit requirements to notify affected taxpayers, aligning FTI breach response with current federal incident response expectations.

changes.title: Supply chain risk management requirements added for FTI systems
changes.newText: Agencies must establish and implement a supply chain risk management (SCRM) plan for systems that process, store, or transmit FTI. The SCRM plan must: identify all third-party vendors and service providers with access to FTI systems; assess and document supply chain risks; establish contractual requirements for vendors to comply with applicable FTI safeguarding provisions; and include procedures for responding to supply chain compromises affecting FTI.
changes.rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 added the SR control family for supply chain risk management. Rev. 9 incorporates these requirements into FTI safeguarding, reflecting the SolarWinds and other supply chain attacks that demonstrated how vendor access can be weaponized to reach sensitive government data. Agencies can no longer limit their security perimeter to their own systems.

changes.title: Cloud service requirements added — FedRAMP Moderate minimum for FTI in cloud
changes.newText: Agencies that process, store, or transmit FTI using cloud services must ensure that the cloud service provider (CSP) has achieved FedRAMP authorization at the Moderate impact level or higher. Agencies must obtain IRS Office of Safeguards approval before migrating FTI workloads to cloud environments. Cloud service agreements must include provisions requiring the CSP to comply with applicable FTI safeguarding requirements and to report security incidents involving FTI within the required timeframes.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8 provided no specific guidance for cloud environments — a significant gap as state agencies rapidly adopted cloud services for tax processing systems. Rev. 9 establishes a clear baseline: FedRAMP Moderate authorization, IRS pre-approval for FTI cloud migrations, and contractual flow-down of safeguarding requirements to CSPs.

changes.title: Privacy controls added — FTI now subject to explicit privacy requirements
changes.area: PT — Personally Identifiable Information Processing and Transparency
changes.newText: Agencies receiving FTI must implement privacy controls consistent with NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 PT control family. Agencies must: document the legal authority for processing FTI under IRC Section 6103; limit processing of FTI to the purposes for which it was received; implement data minimization practices to reduce FTI to the minimum necessary for authorized purposes; and maintain records of FTI disclosures.
changes.rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 integrated privacy controls into the main catalog for the first time. Rev. 9 extends these privacy requirements to FTI safeguarding — reinforcing that FTI is inherently PII and must be handled with the same privacy controls as other sensitive personal information. IRC Section 6103 already restricts FTI use to specific authorized purposes; these controls operationalize that restriction.

changes.title: Account management requirements strengthened — service accounts and privileged access
changes.oldText: Agencies must manage information system accounts, including establishing, activating, modifying, reviewing, disabling, and removing accounts for all users with access to FTI systems. Account reviews must be conducted at least annually.
changes.newText: Agencies must manage all system accounts with access to FTI systems, including individual user accounts, shared accounts, service accounts, system accounts, and privileged accounts. Account reviews must be conducted at least every 90 days for privileged accounts and at least annually for standard user accounts. Service accounts must be inventoried, assigned to an account owner, and reviewed for necessity at least annually. Shared accounts are prohibited for access to FTI except where technically necessary, and such use must be documented and approved.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8's account management requirements were based on NIST Rev 4, which did not specifically address service accounts or privileged account review frequency. Rev. 9 aligns to Rev 5's enhanced AC-2 requirements, adds explicit prohibition on shared accounts for FTI access, and establishes more frequent review cycles for privileged accounts — addressing a common audit finding in IRS safeguards reviews.

changes.title: Audit logging requirements expanded — FTI access events must be captured
changes.oldText: Agencies must enable audit logging on systems that process FTI and retain audit logs for a minimum of three years. Audit logs must capture login and logout events, privileged actions, and access to FTI records.
changes.newText: Agencies must enable comprehensive audit logging on all systems that process, store, or transmit FTI. Audit logs must capture: all authentication events (successful and failed); all access to FTI records including the specific records accessed; privileged user actions; configuration changes; account management events; and system startup and shutdown. Audit logs must be protected from unauthorized modification and deletion. Log retention is a minimum of seven years for FTI-related audit logs, consistent with IRS record retention requirements. Logs must be reviewed at least weekly for anomalous access patterns.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8 required three-year retention and basic event capture. Rev. 9 significantly expands required log content to include specific FTI record access (not just system login), extends retention to seven years to match IRS tax record retention requirements, and adds a weekly log review requirement. This reflects audit findings where FTI breaches were not detected for months or years due to inadequate logging.

changes.title: Media sanitization requirements updated — NIST 800-88 Rev 1 required
changes.oldText: Agencies must sanitize media containing FTI before disposal or reuse using methods consistent with NIST Special Publication 800-88, Guidelines for Media Sanitization.
changes.newText: Agencies must sanitize all media that has contained FTI before disposal or reuse using methods consistent with NIST Special Publication 800-88, Revision 1, Guidelines for Media Sanitization. For media that cannot be effectively sanitized (e.g., certain SSDs, optical media), agencies must destroy the media. Agencies must maintain records of media sanitization and destruction for all media that contained FTI, including the date, method used, and identity of the individual performing sanitization.
changes.rationale: NIST SP 800-88 Revision 1 was published in 2014 and introduced updated guidance for solid-state drives and other modern storage media. Rev. 9 explicitly references the updated standard and adds a documentation requirement for sanitization records — addressing a common gap where agencies could demonstrate a sanitization policy but not evidence of execution.

changes.title: Vulnerability scanning and patching timelines now specified
changes.area: RA — Risk Assessment / SI — System and Information Integrity
changes.newText: Agencies must conduct vulnerability scans of systems processing FTI at least monthly using an authenticated scanning configuration. Critical and high vulnerabilities (CVSS score 7.0 or higher) affecting FTI systems must be remediated within 30 days of discovery. Medium vulnerabilities must be remediated within 90 days. Agencies must document exceptions to patching timelines and obtain supervisory approval for any exception extending beyond the required remediation window.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8 required vulnerability scanning and patch management but did not specify scan frequency or remediation timelines. This created inconsistency in how agencies interpreted the requirement. Rev. 9 establishes specific, measurable timelines aligned with federal vulnerability management benchmarks (CISA BOD 19-02 for internet-facing systems and CISA KEV requirements), making compliance verifiable during IRS safeguards reviews.

changes.title: Remote access controls updated — VPN and zero-trust requirements added
changes.oldText: Agencies must authorize, monitor, and control all remote access to FTI systems. Remote access sessions must use encrypted communications.
changes.newText: Agencies must authorize, monitor, and control all remote access to FTI systems. Remote access must be implemented using encrypted VPN or equivalent zero-trust network access (ZTNA) solution. Remote access sessions must use FIPS 140-3 validated encryption. Agencies must implement session timeout for remote access sessions after no more than 15 minutes of inactivity. Remote access by vendors and contractors must be limited to specific systems and time windows and must be monitored in real time.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8's remote access requirements were minimal. Rev. 9 adds specificity for VPN/ZTNA, session timeout requirements, and vendor remote access controls. The explicit mention of zero-trust network access reflects the federal government's zero-trust mandates (OMB M-22-09) and the reality that VPN alone is insufficient for high-risk remote access scenarios.

changes.title: Insider threat program requirements added
changes.newText: Agencies must implement an insider threat program that includes: procedures for detecting and responding to insider threat indicators related to FTI; training for all personnel with FTI access on insider threat awareness and reporting; technical controls to detect anomalous FTI access patterns; and a process for investigating potential insider threat incidents involving FTI. The insider threat program must be reviewed and updated at least annually.
changes.rationale: Insider threat — whether malicious or negligent — is consistently one of the top causes of FTI breaches identified in IRS safeguards reviews. Rev. 9 makes insider threat program requirements explicit, aligning with the National Insider Threat Policy (NSPD-59/HSPD-24) and NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5's PM-12 control. Agencies must now demonstrate active insider threat detection, not just general access controls.

changes.title: Security awareness training updated — FTI-specific training now required annually
changes.oldText: All personnel with access to FTI must receive security awareness training upon hiring and periodically thereafter. Training must cover the agency's information security policies and the requirements of IRC Section 6103.
changes.newText: All personnel with access to FTI must receive security awareness training prior to being granted FTI access and annually thereafter. Training must specifically cover: the requirements of IRC Section 6103 and penalties for unauthorized disclosure; proper handling and safeguarding of FTI; how to recognize and report phishing, social engineering, and other attack vectors targeting FTI; insider threat awareness and reporting procedures; and the agency's incident response procedures for FTI breaches. Training completion must be documented and verified prior to granting or renewing FTI access.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8 required periodic training but did not specify annual frequency or required content. Rev. 9 mandates annual training with specific FTI-relevant content including phishing awareness and insider threat — reflecting the consistent finding in IRS safeguards audits that personnel handling FTI lacked awareness of their specific obligations and common attack vectors.

changes.title: Annual safeguards review renamed to Safeguards Security Report (SSR) with updated format
changes.oldText: Agencies must complete an annual safeguards review documenting their compliance with IRS Publication 4812 requirements and submit results to the IRS Office of Safeguards.
changes.newText: Agencies must complete an annual Safeguards Security Report (SSR) documenting their compliance with IRS Publication 4812 Rev. 9 requirements. The SSR must be submitted to the IRS Office of Safeguards using the IRS-provided template. The SSR must include: system inventory for all systems processing FTI; results of required security control assessments; open findings and remediation plans with target dates; and certification by agency executive leadership that the information is accurate and complete.
changes.rationale: The annual safeguards review was renamed to Safeguards Security Report (SSR) and standardized with a required IRS template. The addition of executive certification creates accountability at the leadership level — similar to the SOX Section 302 CEO/CFO certification model. The new format also requires more specificity on open findings and remediation timelines, enabling IRS to track systemic issues across agencies.

changes.title: Contingency planning updated — RTO/RPO now required for FTI systems
changes.oldText: Agencies must develop and maintain contingency plans for systems processing FTI, including backup and recovery procedures. Contingency plans must be tested at least annually.
changes.newText: Agencies must develop and maintain contingency plans for all systems processing FTI that include: defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPO) for FTI systems; backup procedures ensuring FTI can be recovered within the defined RPO; recovery procedures with step-by-step instructions for restoring FTI system functionality within the defined RTO; and supply chain contingency considerations for third-party systems supporting FTI processing. Contingency plans must be tested at least annually through a tabletop exercise or functional test, and test results must be documented.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8 required contingency plans and annual testing but did not require specific RTO/RPO targets or supply chain contingency considerations. Rev. 9 adds these requirements, ensuring agencies have measurable recovery objectives and have considered what happens when a vendor supporting FTI processing becomes unavailable.

changes.title: Configuration management baseline required for all FTI systems
changes.newText: Agencies must establish and maintain secure configuration baselines for all systems processing FTI. Baselines must be based on industry-accepted hardening guidance (e.g., DISA STIGs, CIS Benchmarks) appropriate for each system type. Agencies must maintain an accurate inventory of all hardware and software components in FTI systems. Deviations from established baselines must be documented, approved, and monitored. Configuration management must include processes to detect and alert on unauthorized configuration changes.
changes.rationale: Configuration management was implied in Rev. 8 through reference to NIST controls but not explicitly required with specific content. Rev. 9 makes configuration baselines, inventory, and deviation management explicit requirements, directly addressing a top finding in IRS safeguards reviews where agencies lacked documented baselines or allowed configuration drift without detection.

changes.title: Physical access controls updated — visitor logs and escort requirements clarified
changes.oldText: Agencies must control physical access to facilities and systems containing FTI. Access must be limited to authorized personnel.
changes.newText: Agencies must control physical access to all areas where FTI is processed, stored, or transmitted. Physical access controls must include: access authorization records for all individuals with physical access; visitor logs maintained for a minimum of three years; escort requirements for all visitors in areas containing FTI systems; physical access reviews conducted at least quarterly to verify that access authorizations remain appropriate; and controls to prevent unauthorized removal of media or equipment containing FTI.
changes.rationale: Rev. 8's physical security requirements were minimal. Rev. 9 adds specific requirements for visitor logs, escort procedures, and quarterly access reviews — addressing physical security gaps identified in IRS safeguards reviews, particularly at smaller state agencies where physical security policies were informal.

highlights: Publication renamed 'Contractor Security & Privacy Controls' with the PT (privacy) family integrated throughout
First Artificial Intelligence section: statutory definition plus a hard approval gate — no AI on IRS contracts without CO and IRS Cybersecurity approval
Dedicated Cloud Computing section requiring CO and Cybersecurity approval before any cloud use
C-SCRM matured into a defined control level; C-SCRM designation applied to AC-23, AU-13, AU-14, AU-16
Verified against the December 2024 primary text (contributed copy — the IRS overwrites this publication in place)

changes.title: New Section 7.0 — Artificial Intelligence (initial version)
changes.newText: The term artificial intelligence or AI is defined in 15 U.S.C. 9401(3)... Contractors and subcontractors that would like to implement AI technologies within their environments that support the IRS shall contact the COR who will forward the request to the Contracting Officer and IRS Cybersecurity for review and approval. Contractors and subcontractors shall not employ AI technologies to support the IRS contract without approval.
changes.rationale: The December 2024 revision introduced the AI section in its initial form: a statutory definition plus a hard approval gate (CO and IRS Cybersecurity) before any AI use on IRS contracts. The detailed AI policy requirements and public-tool prohibitions came a year later.

changes.title: Publication renamed 'Contractor Security & Privacy Controls'
changes.oldText: Contractor Security Controls
changes.newText: Contractor Security & Privacy Controls
changes.rationale: The rename appears on the December 2024 cover, reflecting integration of the NIST privacy (PT) control family throughout FTI safeguarding requirements.

changes.title: PII Processing and Transparency (PT) family integrated
changes.newText: PT family controls referenced throughout the publication (verified present in the December 2024 text).
changes.rationale: Privacy controls became first-class contractor requirements rather than references, consistent with the SP 800-53 Rev. 5 alignment.

changes.title: C-SCRM designation applied to AC-23, AU-13, AU-14, AU-16
changes.newText: Data Mining Protection, Monitoring for Information Disclosure, Session Audit, and Cross-Organization Audit Logging carry the C-SCRM designation in the December 2024 text.
changes.rationale: Marks these controls as part of the cyber supply chain risk management control level for applicable contractors.

changes.title: Cyber Supply Chain Risk Management expanded throughout
changes.newText: C-SCRM appears extensively across the December 2024 publication (100+ references), including the C-SCRM security control level and SR family requirements.
changes.rationale: Supply chain risk management matured from scattered mentions into a defined control level with its own family, mirroring SP 800-53 Rev. 5.

changes.title: Section 6.0 — Cloud Computing (CO and Cybersecurity approval required)
changes.newText: Contractors that would like to implement cloud computing services within their environments that support the IRS must contact the COR... Contractors must not employ cloud computing services to support the IRS contract without approval from the Contracting Officer and IRS Cybersecurity.
changes.rationale: The December 2024 text contains the dedicated cloud section with the approval requirement. Note: the FedRAMP Authorized Moderate/High mandate for FTI does NOT appear until the December 2025 revision.

changes.title: Mandatory briefing catalog in contractor training (as of July 2023 methodology)
changes.oldText: Generic Security Awareness Training requirement.
changes.newText: As of July 2023, IRS has developed a methodology to assign the following mandatory briefings to all individuals supporting IRS contracts: Annual Cybersecurity Awareness; PIPD; Records Management; Insider Threat Awareness; FMSS Physical Security; IRS Cybersecurity Awareness; Inadvertent Sensitive Information Access; CUI General Awareness; and UNAX.
changes.rationale: The eight-briefing catalog is present in the December 2024 text (dated to a July 2023 methodology, so it may have originated with the September 2023 revision — we can state with certainty only that it is in place by December 2024).

highlights: CONTROL-LEVEL DIFF: every AC-family control compared word-by-word between the December 2024 and December 2025 texts — keyword, phrasing, and requirement changes denoted per control with procedure-impact flags
AC-2 tightened hard: all user accounts disabled after 60 days inactivity (was 120), high-risk users disabled within ONE HOUR (was one day), expired accounts within 3 business days (was undefined)
AC-3: the explicit "Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) shall be implemented" mandate was REMOVED — access enforcement is now model-agnostic
AC-6: partitioning scope extended to PII, new workstation restriction sentence, and separate privileged accounts hardened from "can be implemented" to "shall be implemented"
Publication-wide keyword change: "shall" → "must" and subcontractors now covered via flow-down clauses instead of direct naming
AI section expanded into a full policy regime (ChatGPT/Gemini/Claude named and prohibited) and NEW FedRAMP Moderate/High mandate for FTI in the cloud
Coverage note: control-level comparison verified for the AC family and general sections; remaining families (AT through SR) pending a complete copy of the December 2025 publication

changes.title: AI section massively expanded: policy requirement and public-AI-tool prohibition
changes.oldText: The December 2024 AI section contained the 15 U.S.C. 9401(3) definition and the approval requirement (CO plus IRS Cybersecurity) — roughly one page.
changes.newText: The contractor must define an AI policy for any design, development, acquisition, or use of AI — whether a web-based form, COTS product, or custom tool. Examples now enumerated: Generative AI, Predictive AI, Machine Learning, Large Language Models, voicebots and chatbots, and Robotic Process Automation. Contractors are strictly prohibited from disclosing SBU, including PII and FTI, to publicly available generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Google Bard/Gemini, Anthropic Claude); the IRS treats improper sharing with such tools as an intentional unauthorized disclosure and data breach. IRS data must not be used to train public AI models; minimization and redisclosure safeguards are required when developing AI.
changes.rationale: The single largest change between the December 2024 and December 2025 revisions: AI governance went from an approval gate to a full policy regime with named public tools and breach consequences.

changes.title: FedRAMP mandate for FTI in the cloud
changes.oldText: The December 2024 cloud section required CO and IRS Cybersecurity approval but set no cloud authorization baseline (FedRAMP is not mentioned anywhere in the December 2024 text).
changes.newText: FTI may only be stored, processed, or handled in FedRAMP Authorized cloud environments operating at the Moderate or High impact level.
changes.rationale: A concrete, testable cloud requirement replacing a pure approval process — contractors using non-FedRAMP clouds for FTI now have a defined compliance gap.

changes.title: Training briefing methodology reference updated: July 2023 → July 2025
changes.oldText: As of July 2023, IRS has developed a methodology to assign the following mandatory briefings...
changes.newText: As of July 2025, IRS has developed a methodology to assign the following mandatory briefings... (same eight-briefing catalog).
changes.rationale: Administrative refresh of the methodology date; the briefing list itself is unchanged between the two revisions.

changes.title: AI risk language added: data poisoning, incorrect conclusions, redisclosure
changes.newText: When using AI, contractors must consider the relationship between AI and security and privacy risks, such as collecting more data than is necessary, improper disclosure, misuse, data poisoning and even aspects of incorrect conclusions.
changes.rationale: New risk-consideration language specific to AI systems, absent from the December 2024 text.

changes.title: Publication-wide keyword change: 'shall' → 'must'; 'contractors and subcontractors' → 'Contractors'
changes.oldText: Requirements phrased as "The contractor and subcontractor shall..." throughout the control catalog.
changes.newText: Requirements phrased as "The Contractor must..." throughout; subcontractor obligations are carried by the flow-down requirement in each control ("Contractors must require their prime contractors to implement this control and flow down this requirement to relevant sub-tier contractors").
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — LOW but broad: policy documents that quote 4812 control language verbatim should be updated to the new must-phrasing. The binding effect is unchanged; subcontractor coverage now runs through flow-down clauses rather than direct naming in each control statement.

changes.title: AC-2 Account Management — inactivity disablement tightened: 120 days → 60 days for all user accounts
changes.oldText: The information system shall automatically disable user accounts after 120 days of inactivity and shall automatically disable administrator accounts after 60 days. Contractors using a CSP shall ensure that they, or the CSP will review privileged accounts semi-annually and automatically disable inactive privileged accounts after 60 days; user accounts annually and automatically disable inactive user accounts after 90 days.
changes.newText: The Contractor or CSP must automatically disable all user accounts after 60 days of inactivity.
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — HIGH: the standard-user inactivity threshold is cut in half (120 → 60 days) and the separate CSP review scheme (60/90-day split) is consolidated to a single 60-day rule for all accounts. Update account lifecycle procedures and automated disablement jobs.

changes.title: AC-2 Account Management — high-risk user disablement: one day → one hour
changes.oldText: Accounts of users identified as posing a high-risk shall have their accounts disabled within one day of discovery of the risk.
changes.newText: The Contractor must disable any account within one hour if the user poses a serious security or privacy risk, either by planning to misuse access or by being vulnerable to compromise.
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — HIGH: a 24x tightening of the response window. Incident response and HR-security coordination procedures must support one-hour account disablement, which effectively requires an on-call path and pre-authorized disable authority.

changes.title: AC-2 Account Management — expired/orphaned accounts: defined 3-business-day deadline
changes.oldText: The contractor shall disable accounts within the information system when the accounts have expired or are no longer associated with a user (no timeframe stated).
changes.newText: The Contractor must disable accounts within 3 business days when the accounts have expired or are no longer associated with a user.
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — MEDIUM: an undefined obligation became a testable deadline. Joiner-mover-leaver procedures need a measurable 3-business-day SLA.

changes.title: AC-2 Account Management — explicit dual-account requirement for privileged users no longer stated
changes.oldText: Contractor personnel who perform privileged roles such as System Administrators (SA) shall have two user accounts: one for privileged duties and one for general user activity. Each account shall be independent of the other, be limited in use, and shall be used solely for its defined purpose.
changes.newText: The explicit dual-account paragraph does not appear in the December 2025 AC-2 text. (Note: AC-6 still requires separate accounts for privileged users — the substance survives there, now phrased as "shall be implemented".)
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — LOW: keep dual accounts; the requirement moved rather than disappeared. Cross-reference AC-6 in your control mapping.

changes.title: AC-2 Account Management — unique account per user and account manager made explicit
changes.oldText: Whenever there is more than one contractor using an IT asset, such as a server, network, or information system, the contractor shall assign an account manager (context-dependent phrasing).
changes.newText: The Contractor or CSP shall create a unique account for each user who accesses the information system. The Contractor or CSP shall assign an account manager to manage accounts and employ automated mechanisms to support the management of accounts.
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — LOW/MEDIUM: unique-account-per-user is now an unconditional lead requirement; shared accounts anywhere in scope are a clear finding.

changes.title: AC-3 Access Enforcement — explicit RBAC mandate removed
changes.oldText: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) shall be implemented.
changes.newText: The December 2025 AC-3 text contains no RBAC sentence (zero RBAC mentions in the December 2025 publication; two in December 2024). Access enforcement, approval-before-access, consequence framework, and flow-down requirements remain.
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — MEDIUM: RBAC is no longer named as the mandatory model — access enforcement is model-agnostic. Organizations using ABAC or hybrid models gain flexibility; existing RBAC implementations remain compliant. Do not remove RBAC controls without confirming your contract language.

changes.title: AC-4 Information Flow Enforcement — explicit enforcement-of-approved-authorizations requirement added
changes.oldText: Contractors and subcontractors, including those using a CSP, shall ensure that they implement information flow enforcement (with examples).
changes.newText: Contractor information systems must enforce approved authorizations for controlling the flow of information within the system and between connected systems based on applicable policies and agreements — added ahead of the existing examples. The section also now carries supplemental C-SCRM separation-of-duties guidance for acquisition decisions (denying developers the privilege to promote code, etc.).
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — MEDIUM: flow control must trace to approved authorizations (documented policy basis per flow), not just technical restrictions. Data-flow documentation should map each allowed flow to its authorizing policy or agreement.

changes.title: AC-6 Least Privilege — sensitive-data partitioning extended to PII; workstation restriction added; separate privileged accounts hardened from guidance to requirement
changes.oldText: FTI and IRS SBU data shall be physically or logically partitioned... Accounts with administrative privileges shall be prohibited from web browsing... This can be implemented by establishing separate accounts for privileged users. Restrict File Transfer Protocol (FTP) or Telnet.
changes.newText: FTI, PII and IRS SBU data must be physically or logically partitioned... Workstations shall be configured to restrict logical access to IRS SBU (new sentence). This shall be implemented by establishing separate accounts for privileged users ("can" → "shall"). Restrict access to insecure protocols such as FTP or Telnet (broadened category).
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — MEDIUM/HIGH: three real changes inside one control — PII added to the partitioning scope, a new workstation-level access restriction, and separate privileged accounts changing from an example ("can") to a mandate ("shall"). The protocol restriction is now a category (insecure protocols), not just two named ones.

changes.title: AC-7 Unsuccessful Login Attempts — lockout requirement consolidated (same values, cleaner rule)
changes.oldText: The contractor and subcontractor shall configure their information system to enforce a limit of three consecutive invalid logon attempts by a user. Upon a 3rd unsuccessful logon attempt, in a 120-minute period, the user's account shall be automatically locked. The account is to remain locked for 15 minutes or until unlocked by an administrator or password reset program.
changes.newText: The Contractor must configure their information system to lock an account after 3 unsuccessful logon attempts in a 120-minute period. Once an account is locked it must remain locked for 15 minutes or until released by an administrator or password reset program.
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — NONE (verify only): thresholds unchanged (3 attempts / 120-minute window / 15-minute lock; CSP rule unchanged). Phrasing consolidated — confirm your documented parameter values still match.

changes.title: AC-18 Wireless Access — personally provided wireless at home/alternate work sites explicitly in scope
changes.oldText: Wireless requirements addressed contractor systems; personally provided wireless capabilities were not explicitly called out.
changes.newText: This includes contractor personnel utilizing personally provided wireless capabilities to access contractor systems remotely from home and alternative work locations (new sentence). "Only users identified and explicitly authorized" may configure wireless ("independently configure" simplified to "configure").
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — MEDIUM: home Wi-Fi used to reach contractor systems handling SBU is now explicitly inside the control scope. Remote work and wireless policies should state authentication/encryption expectations for personal wireless use.

changes.title: AC-22 Publicly Accessible Content — condensed; "individuals" → "personnel"
changes.oldText: Contractors and subcontractors, including those using a CSP shall designate individuals authorized to post information onto a publicly accessible information system; train authorized individuals; and review the proposed content of information prior to posting (bulleted duties).
changes.newText: Contractors, including those using a CSP must ensure that they designate, and train personnel authorized to post information onto a publicly accessible information system as allowed by the IRS or contract (consolidated statement).
changes.rationale: PROCEDURE IMPACT — LOW: duties condensed into one statement; keep your pre-posting content review in procedure — the consolidated text folds review into the designate-and-train obligation.
